title: Burton Albion F.C.

is a professional [association football](/wiki/Association_football "Association football") club in the town of [Burton upon Trent](/wiki/Burton_upon_Trent "Burton upon Trent"), [Staffordshire](/wiki/Staffordshire "Staffordshire"), England. The team competes in [League One](/wiki/EFL_League_One "EFL League One"), the third level of the [English football league system](/wiki/English_football_league_system "English football league system"). The club moved its home ground in 2005 to the [Pirelli Stadium](/wiki/Pirelli_Stadium "Pirelli Stadium") from [Eton Park](/wiki/Eton_Park "Eton Park"). The club's nickname, The Brewers, evokes the [brewing heritage](/wiki/Brewers_of_Burton "Brewers of Burton") of Burton upon Trent.

**Burton Albion Football Club** is a professional [association football](/wiki/Association_football "Association football") club in the town of [Burton upon Trent](/wiki/Burton_upon_Trent "Burton upon Trent"), [Staffordshire](/wiki/Staffordshire "Staffordshire"), England. The team competes in [League One](/wiki/EFL_League_One "EFL League One"), the third level of the [English football league system](/wiki/English_football_league_system "English football league system"). The club moved its home ground in 2005 to the [Pirelli Stadium](/wiki/Pirelli_Stadium "Pirelli Stadium") from [Eton Park](/wiki/Eton_Park "Eton Park"). The club's nickname, The Brewers, evokes the [brewing heritage](/wiki/Brewers_of_Burton "Brewers of Burton") of Burton upon Trent.

Burton Albion were formed in 1950 and initially joined the [Birmingham & District League](/wiki/West_Midlands_(Regional)_League "West Midlands (Regional) League") before switching to the [Southern League](/wiki/Southern_Football_League "Southern Football League") eight years later. They were promoted from the Southern League Division One in 1965–66, 1971–72 and 1973–74 and were relegated from the Southern League Premier Division in 1970, 1973 and 1977. Burton spent 1979 to 1987 in the [Northern Premier League](/wiki/Northern_Premier_League "Northern Premier League"), before reverting to the Southern League Premier Division. The club rejoined the Northern Premier League in 2001 and were promoted to the [Conference](/wiki/National_League_(division) "National League (division)") as Northern Premier League champions in 2001–02.

[Nigel Clough](/wiki/Nigel_Clough "Nigel Clough") spent seven seasons as the club's player-manager in the Conference and then led them into the [Football League](/wiki/English_Football_League "English Football League") as champions of the Conference in 2008–09. They lost the [2014 League Two play-off final](/wiki/2014_Football_League_Two_play-off_final "2014 Football League Two play-off final"), but went on to win the League Two title in 2014–15 and were promoted from League One in 2015–16. Burton spent two seasons in the [Championship](/wiki/EFL_Championship "EFL Championship") until relegation in 2018.

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Burton_Albion_F.C.&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

### 1950–1998: early years

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Burton_Albion_F.C.&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: 1950–1998: early years")]

Burton Albion were formed in 1950, and joined the [Birmingham & District League](/wiki/West_Midlands_(Regional)_League "West Midlands (Regional) League").[[1]](#cite_note-fchd-1) They finished the 1953–54 season as runners-up, and in 1958–59 joined the [Southern League](/wiki/Southern_Football_League "Southern Football League") North Western zone.[[1]](#cite_note-fchd-1) In 1965–66, Burton missed out on the runners-up place on [goal difference](/wiki/Goal_difference "Goal difference"), but were still promoted to the Southern League Premier Division.[[1]](#cite_note-fchd-1) They avoided relegation in 1968, due to [Stevenage Town](/wiki/Stevenage_Town_F.C. "Stevenage Town F.C.") folding, but were relegated to Division One after an unsuccessful 1969–70 season.[[1]](#cite_note-fchd-1)

Burton missed out on promotion on goal average in 1970–71, but finished as runners-up the following season and were promoted back to the Premier Division.[[1]](#cite_note-fchd-1) The next two seasons saw them relegated, and then promoted back to the Premier Division again.[[1]](#cite_note-fchd-1) They stayed in the same division until being relegated once more at the end of 1976–77.[[1]](#cite_note-fchd-1) League rearrangements saw Burton moved to the [Northern Premier League](/wiki/Northern_Premier_League "Northern Premier League"), because of their location in the central [Midlands](/wiki/Midlands_of_England "Midlands of England"), and then back to the Southern League in 1987–88, the season after losing in a replayed FA Trophy Final to [Kidderminster Harriers](/wiki/Kidderminster_Harriers_F.C. "Kidderminster Harriers F.C.").[[1]](#cite_note-fchd-1)

### Into the new millennium

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Burton_Albion_F.C.&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Into the new millennium")]

In October 1998, [Nigel Clough](/wiki/Nigel_Clough "Nigel Clough") was appointed as player-manager[[2]](#cite_note-2) and he led the club to two successive runners-up spots, in 1999–2000 and 2000–01.[[1]](#cite_note-fchd-1) Burton were again moved to the Northern Premier League Premier Division in 2001–02, which they won by a margin of 15 points, scoring 106 goals in the process.[[3]](#cite_note-3) The club were promoted to the [Football Conference](/wiki/Football_Conference "Football Conference") for the first time.[[1]](#cite_note-fchd-1)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e9/BurtonvGills.jpg/250px-BurtonvGills.jpg)](/wiki/File:BurtonvGills.jpg)

Albion in action against [Gillingham](/wiki/Gillingham_F.C. "Gillingham F.C.") in the final match of the [2012–13](/wiki/2012%E2%80%9313_in_English_football "2012–13 in English football") season

The club was brought to national attention when they were drawn at home against eleven-times winners [Manchester United](/wiki/Manchester_United_F.C. "Manchester United F.C.") in the [third round](/wiki/FA_Cup_2005-06#Third_round_proper "FA Cup 2005-06") of the 2005–06 [FA Cup](/wiki/FA_Cup "FA Cup"). The Brewers held the Premier League team to a 0–0 draw at home, but lost the replay 5–0 in front of over 11,000 Burton fans, setting a record for number of away fans at [Old Trafford](/wiki/Old_Trafford "Old Trafford").[[4]](#cite_note-4)

### 2009–present: Football League

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Burton_Albion_F.C.&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: 2009–present: Football League")]

In January 2009, with Burton 13 points clear at the top of the table, Clough left the club to become the manager of [Derby County](/wiki/Derby_County_F.C. "Derby County F.C."), with [Roy McFarland](/wiki/Roy_McFarland "Roy McFarland") installed as caretaker manager until the end of the season. Despite this managerial change, Burton went on to set a league record for the most consecutive wins, and in February 2009, when the team was 19 points clear at the top of the table, Conference sponsors Blue Square declared Burton the winners of the 2008–09 title in a public relations stunt, in which they paid out all bets. Following that announcement, the club saw their lead reduced week by week, but secured promotion to the [Football League](/wiki/The_Football_League "The Football League") in the final game of the season, despite losing 2–1 away to [Torquay United](/wiki/Torquay_United_F.C. "Torquay United F.C."), when [Cambridge United](/wiki/Cambridge_United_F.C. "Cambridge United F.C.") could only manage a goalless draw to [Altrincham](/wiki/Altrincham_F.C. "Altrincham F.C."). At the end of the season Roy McFarland left the club and was replaced by [Paul Peschisolido](/wiki/Paul_Peschisolido "Paul Peschisolido"), with [Gary Rowett](/wiki/Gary_Rowett "Gary Rowett") acting as his assistant.

Burton's first win in the Football League was 5–2 against [Morecambe](/wiki/Morecambe_F.C. "Morecambe F.C.") at the Pirelli Stadium, and they finished 13th in their first campaign in the Football League. In their second season, Burton claimed a notable scalp in the [FA Cup](/wiki/FA_Cup "FA Cup") [third round](/wiki/FA_Cup_2010%E2%80%9311#Third_round_proper "FA Cup 2010–11") when they knocked out championship team Middlesbrough 2–1 at the Pirelli Stadium.[[5]](#cite_note-5) In the league, Burton experienced a 17-game winless run and fell from fifth place on [Boxing Day](/wiki/Boxing_Day "Boxing Day") to 17th place at the end of the [2011–12 season](/wiki/2011-12_Football_League_Two "2011-12 Football League Two"), which led to the sacking of Peschisolido.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/bc/Burton_Albion_FC_League_Performance.svg/500px-Burton_Albion_FC_League_Performance.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Burton_Albion_FC_League_Performance.svg)

Chart of yearly table positions of Burton Albion in the football league.

[Gary Rowett](/wiki/Gary_Rowett "Gary Rowett") was appointed as the new manager of Burton in May 2012.[[6]](#cite_note-6) In his first full season in charge, he led Burton to a fourth-place finish and the play-offs, missing out on automatic promotion by two points.[[7]](#cite_note-7) Burton lost their play-off semi-final 4–5 on aggregate to [Bradford City](/wiki/Bradford_City_A.F.C. "Bradford City A.F.C.") despite winning the first leg 3–2 at [Valley Parade](/wiki/Valley_Parade "Valley Parade").[[8]](#cite_note-8) In the [2013–14](/wiki/2013-14_Football_League_Two "2013-14 Football League Two") season, Burton finished sixth, reaching the [play-off final](/wiki/2014_Football_League_Two_play-off_Final "2014 Football League Two play-off Final") in which they lost 1–0 against [Fleetwood Town](/wiki/Fleetwood_Town "Fleetwood Town").[[9]](#cite_note-9)

During the 2014–15 season, Rowett left to join [Birmingham City](/wiki/Birmingham_City_F.C. "Birmingham City F.C."), and was replaced by [Jimmy Floyd Hasselbaink](/wiki/Jimmy_Floyd_Hasselbaink "Jimmy Floyd Hasselbaink").[[10]](#cite_note-10) Under Hasselbaink, the Brewers won [League Two](/wiki/Football_League_Two "Football League Two") and were promoted to [League One](/wiki/Football_League_One "Football League One") for the first time in their history.[[11]](#cite_note-Morecambe_1–2_Burton-11)[[12]](#cite_note-12) Hasselbaink left by mutual consent in December 2015 to join [Queens Park Rangers](/wiki/Queens_Park_Rangers_F.C. "Queens Park Rangers F.C.") as manager. Clough returned to Burton to replace him for his second spell as manager and led the club to a second-place finish in the league, earning promotion to the [Football League Championship](/wiki/Football_League_Championship "Football League Championship"), another first for Burton.[[13]](#cite_note-13)

The Brewers opened their first season in the championship with a 4–3 loss to local rivals Nottingham Forest.[[14]](#cite_note-14) Burton went on a six-match streak without losing between 18 February and 18 March, beginning with a 2–1 win at home to ex-Premier League opponents Norwich City and culminating in a 3–5 defeat to Brentford and including a 1–0 win over Nottingham Forest in the reverse fixture.[[15]](#cite_note-15) Burton secured their championship status on 29 April 2017 after a 1–1 draw with [Barnsley](/wiki/Barnsley_F.C. "Barnsley F.C.").[[16]](#cite_note-16) Burton spent much of their second season in the championship in the relegation zone. three wins in the late stage of the season boosted their chances of survival, including a 2–1 win over relegation rivals [Sunderland](/wiki/Sunderland_A.F.C "Sunderland A.F.C").[[17]](#cite_note-17) However, following a 2–1 defeat to [Preston North End](/wiki/Preston_North_End_F.C. "Preston North End F.C.") on the final day of the season, Burton were relegated back to League One.[[18]](#cite_note-18)

In [2018–19](/wiki/2018%E2%80%9319_Burton_Albion_F.C._season "2018–19 Burton Albion F.C. season"), in spite of being in mid-table in League One, they made the semi-finals of the [EFL Cup](/wiki/2018%E2%80%9319_EFL_Cup "2018–19 EFL Cup"), after wins over [Shrewsbury Town](/wiki/Shrewsbury_Town_F.C. "Shrewsbury Town F.C."), [Aston Villa](/wiki/Aston_Villa_F.C. "Aston Villa F.C."), [Burnley](/wiki/Burnley_F.C. "Burnley F.C."), [Nottingham Forest](/wiki/Nottingham_Forest_F.C. "Nottingham Forest F.C.") and [Middlesbrough](/wiki/Middlesbrough_F.C. "Middlesbrough F.C.").[[19]](#cite_note-19) Facing [Manchester City](/wiki/Manchester_City_F.C. "Manchester City F.C."), Burton lost the first leg 9–0 at the Etihad Stadium, eventually losing 10–0 on aggregate.[[20]](#cite_note-20)[[21]](#cite_note-21)

[2019–20](/wiki/2019%E2%80%9320_Burton_Albion_F.C._season "2019–20 Burton Albion F.C. season") saw Burton reach the fourth round of the EFL Cup, defeating Premier League club [AFC Bournemouth](/wiki/AFC_Bournemouth "AFC Bournemouth") before losing 3–1 at home to [Leicester City](/wiki/Leicester_City_F.C. "Leicester City F.C.").[[22]](#cite_note-22) The final nine games of the season would be permanently suspended due to the [COVID-19 pandemic](/wiki/COVID-19_pandemic "COVID-19 pandemic"), with Burton finishing the season in 12th place. The summer of 2020 saw significant change with [Nigel Clough](/wiki/Nigel_Clough "Nigel Clough") stepping down as manager, bringing to an end a 228-game long second spell at the club and surpassing 900 games in total with the Brewers.[[23]](#cite_note-23) His successor, [Jake Buxton](/wiki/Jake_Buxton "Jake Buxton"), struggled to fill the void and was sacked on 29 December 2020 after winning just two of his 21 league matches, and with Burton sat six points from safety at the bottom of the League One table.[[24]](#cite_note-24) The club would soon respond by announcing the return of [Jimmy Floyd Hasselbaink](/wiki/Jimmy_Floyd_Hasselbaink "Jimmy Floyd Hasselbaink") as manager.[[25]](#cite_note-25) Tasked with securing survival, the new management team completed a remarkable turnaround, climbing out of the relegation zone by the start of March and finishing 16th, 10 points above the bottom four. The turnaround included a club-record run of six consecutive [EFL](/wiki/English_Football_League "English Football League") victories.[[26]](#cite_note-26)

After a disastrous start to [2022–23](/wiki/2022%E2%80%9323_Burton_Albion_F.C._season "2022–23 Burton Albion F.C. season"), Hasselbaink resigned as Burton Albion manager.[[27]](#cite_note-27) Dino Maamria took over the reins of a club that had just one point after seven matches, successfully guiding the club to survival in 15th place.[[28]](#cite_note-28)

Dino Maamria's tenure as manager would end in December 2023,[[29]](#cite_note-29) and was succeeded by [Martin Paterson](/wiki/Martin_Paterson "Martin Paterson").[[30]](#cite_note-30) The Brewers ultimately avoided relegation on the final day of the season, however, Paterson would depart the club at the end of the season.[[31]](#cite_note-31)[[32]](#cite_note-32) with first team coaches Gary Mills and John Dreyer also departing the same day.[[33]](#cite_note-33)

In June 2024, Chairman Ben Robinson sold the entire majority stake in the football club to the Nordic Football Group (NFG).[[34]](#cite_note-34) NFG's acquisition of Burton Albion includes a large group of investors from the [Nordic](/wiki/Nordic_countries "Nordic countries") countries. The following day, NFG made their first appointment, announcing the arrival of [Chelsea U21](/wiki/Chelsea_F.C._Development_Squad_and_Academy "Chelsea F.C. Development Squad and Academy") head coach [Mark Robinson](/wiki/Mark_Robinson_(football_manager) "Mark Robinson (football manager)").[[35]](#cite_note-35) After a poor start of 11 league games without a win, Robinson was sacked and replaced with interim boss [Tom Hounsell](/wiki/Tom_Hounsell "Tom Hounsell"), who picked up the clubs first league win of the season at the 14th attempt and after 3 months of trying[[36]](#cite_note-36)

The [2025-26](/wiki/2025%E2%80%9326_Burton_Albion_F.C._season "2025–26 Burton Albion F.C. season") season saw Burton reach the [fourth round](/wiki/2025-26_fa_cup#fourth_round "2025-26 fa cup") of the [FA Cup](/wiki/FA_Cup "FA Cup") for just the second time in their history, rewarding them a tie at home to [West Ham United](/wiki/West_Ham_United_F.C. "West Ham United F.C.").

## Stadium

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Burton_Albion_F.C.&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Stadium")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/9c/Burton_Albion_FC%2C_Pirelli_Stadium%2C_Burton_upon_Trent%2C_Staffordshire_-_geograph.org.uk_-_190956.jpg/250px-Burton_Albion_FC%2C_Pirelli_Stadium%2C_Burton_upon_Trent%2C_Staffordshire_-_geograph.org.uk_-_190956.jpg)](/wiki/File:Burton_Albion_FC,_Pirelli_Stadium,_Burton_upon_Trent,_Staffordshire_-_geograph.org.uk_-_190956.jpg)

[Pirelli Stadium](/wiki/Pirelli_Stadium "Pirelli Stadium")

Albion began life at the Lloyds Foundry ground on Wellington Street, but high attendances meant that the club quickly searched for a more suitable home. [Eton Park](/wiki/Eton_Park "Eton Park") was built off Derby Road and officially opened on 20 September 1958, coinciding with the club's promotion to the [Southern League](/wiki/Southern_Football_League "Southern Football League"). Until its demolition in 2005, the Brewers played all their home games at Eton Park.

The [Pirelli Stadium](/wiki/Pirelli_Stadium "Pirelli Stadium") on Princess Way was built in 2005 and is the current home of the Brewers, replacing Eton Park, also on the same road, which was demolished and developed into housing.[[37]](#cite_note-chairman-37) The ground cost £7.2 million to build, and was built on the former site of the Pirelli UK Tyres Ltd Sports & Social Club. The land was donated to the club by [Pirelli](/wiki/Pirelli "Pirelli") in return for naming rights.[[37]](#cite_note-chairman-37)

The ground was designed by architect Jon Hawkeye, and has served as the inspiration for numerous newer grounds, including [Morecambe](/wiki/Morecambe_F.C. "Morecambe F.C.")'s [Globe Arena](/wiki/Globe_Arena_(football_stadium) "Globe Arena (football stadium)") and the proposed [Hayes & Yeading](/wiki/Hayes_%26_Yeading_F.C. "Hayes & Yeading F.C.") stadium.[[38]](#cite_note-38) It gained its most recent safety certificate from [Staffordshire County Council](/wiki/Staffordshire_County_Council "Staffordshire County Council") on 12 July 2010,[[39]](#cite_note-safety-39) having been subject to crowd trouble on 8 May 2010 at the hands of [Grimsby Town](/wiki/Grimsby_Town_F.C. "Grimsby Town F.C.") fans following their relegation from [Football League Two](/wiki/Football_League_Two "Football League Two").[[40]](#cite_note-40)

The Pirelli Stadium has seen minor capacity changes since its construction, and the current capacity is 6,912, with 2,034 being seated in the South (Main) Stand. The current record attendance for the stadium stands at 6,746 for an [EFL Championship](/wiki/EFL_Championship "EFL Championship") match against [Derby County](/wiki/Derby_County_F.C. "Derby County F.C.") on 26 August 2016.[[41]](#cite_note-41) Previous records include 6,192 for a [Conference National](/wiki/Conference_National "Conference National") 1–0 defeat against [Oxford United](/wiki/Oxford_United_F.C. "Oxford United F.C."), during the club's [title-winning season](/wiki/2008%E2%80%9309_in_English_football "2008–09 in English football"),[[42]](#cite_note-oxford-42) and 6,191 for an [FA Cup](/wiki/FA_Cup "FA Cup") third-round match on 8 January 2006 against [Manchester United](/wiki/Manchester_United_F.C. "Manchester United F.C.").[[43]](#cite_note-manchester-43)

The stadium also hosts the National ISFA Under-13 tournament final.

## Rivalries

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Burton_Albion_F.C.&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Rivalries")]

In their non-League days the Brewers' local rivals were [Gresley Rovers](/wiki/Gresley_Rovers_F.C. "Gresley Rovers F.C."),[[44]](#cite_note-44) [Nuneaton Borough](/wiki/Nuneaton_Borough_F.C. "Nuneaton Borough F.C."), [Stafford Rangers](/wiki/Stafford_Rangers_F.C. "Stafford Rangers F.C.")[[45]](#cite_note-45) and [Tamworth](/wiki/Tamworth_F.C. "Tamworth F.C."). However, since the club's rise to the [Football League](/wiki/Football_League "Football League") these rivalries have become less intense.

Following promotion to the Football League, local rivalries with [Port Vale](/wiki/Port_Vale_F.C. "Port Vale F.C."), [Notts County](/wiki/Notts_County_F.C. "Notts County F.C."), [Chesterfield](/wiki/Chesterfield_F.C. "Chesterfield F.C.") and [Walsall](/wiki/Walsall_F.C. "Walsall F.C.") have arisen. Except for Notts County, all these clubs had been rivals to Albion's predecessor club, [Burton United](/wiki/Burton_United_F.C. "Burton United F.C."), in the early 1900s. There is also a largely friendly rivalry with near neighbours [Derby County](/wiki/Derby_County_F.C. "Derby County F.C."), partly because of the shared fanbase, but particularly following the transfer of manager Nigel Clough to the [championship](/wiki/Football_League_Championship "Football League Championship") club in 2009 and the arrival of several ex-Derby players during the [Paul Peschisolido](/wiki/Paul_Peschisolido "Paul Peschisolido") era.

Their 2016 promotion into the championship resulted in matches with local big clubs [Aston Villa](/wiki/Aston_Villa_F.C. "Aston Villa F.C."), [Birmingham City](/wiki/Birmingham_City_F.C. "Birmingham City F.C."), [Nottingham Forest](/wiki/Nottingham_Forest_F.C. "Nottingham Forest F.C.") and [Wolverhampton Wanderers](/wiki/Wolverhampton_Wanderers_F.C. "Wolverhampton Wanderers F.C.").
